---
pagetitle: "CDIO BI"
output-file: index.html
lang: en
title-theme: "in Business Intelligence"
subtitle-highlight: "Designing Authentic Industry-Engaged Assessment for Professional Competence"
presenters:
  - name: "Dr. Helga Ingimundardóttir"
    hi-username: "helgaingim"
    orcid: "0000-0002-2780-3546"
    github: "tungufoss"
subtitle-position: below
event-meta: "22<sup>nd</sup> International CDIO Conference"
event-date: "June 23<sup>rd</sup>, 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: styles/hi.scss
    css:
      - styles/watermark.css
    filters:
      - _extensions/hi-title/hi-title.lua
      - _extensions/card-enum/card-enum.lua
    logo: img/hi/hi_named_logo-en.svg
    footer: "22<sup>nd</sup> International CDIO Conference · Helga Ingimundardóttir · helgaingim@hi.is"
    include-in-header: partials/header-includes.inc
    include-after-body: partials/body-after.inc
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: speaker
    controls: true
    transition: none
    background-transition: none
    width: 1280
    height: 720
---

{{< hi-title >}}

::: {.notes}
I'm **Helga Ingimundardóttir** from the **University of Iceland** — this is my first time at CDIO, and I'm presenting **twice this week**.

- **Today**: *why* we redesigned and what the design looks like
- **Tomorrow** (June 24): *how* — pull requests as assessment infrastructure
- Relevant if you do **industry-engaged** or **project-based** courses
:::

## Motivation

::: {.fa-card cols=1}
- building-columns | Academia | aims to prepare students for professional practice, but offers limited guidance on how to operationalize this in course design and assessment
- industry | Industry | expects credible, reproducible results supported by clear reasoning and usable for decision-making
- scale-balanced | **Underlying mismatch** | what counts as "good work" differs between academic and professional contexts
:::

::: {.notes}
Everyone agrees academia should prepare students for professional practice — the question is **how to actually design for it**.

- This isn't a **values** mismatch — it's a **design** mismatch
- Industry needs work that is **credible, reproducible, decision-usable**
- Our own course: the partner valued the collaboration but **would not use the results**; students found it **heavy and uneven**
- Core question: how do we design so work is **authentic in practice** *and* **meaningful for learning**?
:::

---

## Context: Business Intelligence

::: {.fa-card cols=2}
- magnifying-glass-chart | **Overview** | | * 6 ECTS course | * advanced undergraduate and early graduate students | * 10–15 students, two teams
- database | **What is BI?** | data-driven decision support | * data analysis | * systems integration | * communication to inform organizational decisions
:::

::: {.statement}
Goal: **strengthen industrial relevance and professional competence**
:::

::: {.notes}
Business Intelligence is about **decision support** — not just producing correct results, but communicating **assumptions, limitations, and implications**.

- **6 ECTS**, advanced undergrad and early grad, **10–15 students** in two teams
- Small enough for **sustained industry engagement** — that's the key
:::

---

## Industry-engaged project

::: {.fa-card cols=1}
- handshake | **Sustained industrial context** | a single dataset and domain used across the entire semester
- industry | **Industry partner role** | | * problem framing | * milestone discussions | * final evaluation
- scale-balanced | **Dual context** | academic and industry perspectives coexist throughout
:::

::: {.notes}
The biggest single structural change was using **one dataset, one domain, all semester**.

- No repeated onboarding — students build **depth** instead of breadth
- Industry partner present at **problem framing → milestones → final evaluation**
- That continuity is what creates conditions for **usable results**
:::

---

## Design focus: LOUIS-guided

::: {.statement}
Focus on a small set of competences within a sustained industry context.
:::

[[**LOUIS**](https://aurora-universities.eu/louis/) — Learning Outcomes in University for Impact on Society (Aurora University Network). Focus on a small set of competences (max 3).]{.lean-in}

![](include/lcf-grid.svg){style="width:100%;max-height:390px;object-fit:contain;display:block;margin:auto"}

::: {.notes}
To focus the redesign, I used the **LOUIS** framework — Learning Outcomes in University for Impact on Society — developed by the **Aurora University Network**, a European alliance of nine research universities of which the **University of Iceland is a member**. Aurora runs **faculty workshops** to help teachers embed these competences into their courses — which is exactly what I did.

- Key workshop insight: **don't spread thin** — pick at most **three competences** and make expectations explicit
- We chose: **Information literacy · Problem solving · Teamwork**
:::

---

## Pedagogical structure

![](include/course-flow.svg){width=100% style="display:block;margin:auto;max-height:420px"}

```{=html}
<p style="text-align:center;margin-top:0.25em;margin-bottom:0;font-size:13px;color:var(--muted);font-style:italic">
  <i class="fa-solid fa-user-plus"></i> <strong>Team-Based Learning:</strong>
  <i class="fa-solid fa-user"></i> iRAT (individual readiness) &nbsp;·&nbsp;
  <i class="fa-solid fa-users"></i> tRAT (team readiness) &nbsp;·&nbsp;
  <i class="fa-solid fa-users"></i> tAPP (team application) &nbsp;·&nbsp;
  <i class="fa-solid fa-user"></i> iREF (individual reflection)
</p>
<p style="text-align:center;margin-top:0.05em;margin-bottom:0;font-size:13px;color:var(--muted);font-style:italic">
  <i class="fa-solid fa-shuffle"></i> <strong>Flipped classroom</strong> &nbsp;·&nbsp;
  <i class="fa-solid fa-repeat"></i> <strong>Iterative modules</strong> leading into capstone &nbsp;·&nbsp;
  <i class="fa-solid fa-circle-info"></i> iRAT/tRAT use is slightly unconventional
</p>
```

::: {.notes}
Every module follows the same rhythm: **prepare → align → apply → reflect** — this is exactly what students will meet in the workplace as the **Scrum process**: sprint planning, sprint, retrospective. The course structure itself is professional practice.

- **iRAT / tRAT**: readiness checks — everyone arrives prepared
- **tAPP**: team applies to the shared project — where the real work happens
- **iREF**: individual reflection — what changed, what you'd do differently
- After all modules, a **mini-capstone** bridges to the final capstone — which is not a new project but a revisit and refinement of everything that came before
:::

---

## Assessment as development

[_Core mechanism_]{.slide-subtitle}

::: {.fa-card cols=1}
- list-check | **One rubric, many iterations** | same assessment rubric reused across all modules and the capstone | * analytical soundness | * transparency | * reproducibility | * stakeholder relevance
- forward | **Developmental shift** | feedback is \_carried forward\_ — not reset between tasks
:::

[Standards fixed; performance expectations scaled during the learning phase.]{.lean-in}

::: {.notes}
Students see the **final rubric from day one** — the standard doesn't change, but expectations are **scaled** during the learning phase.

- In practice this means **pull requests**: changes proposed, reviewed, integrated — or explicitly deferred
- Unresolved issues **stay visible** across submissions — nothing disappears
- Same rubric reused → **grading is faster**; I can see exactly what changed
:::

---

## Evolving feedback

::: {.fa-card cols=3}
- chalkboard-user | **Instructor** | | * method | * implementation
- industry | **Industry partner** | | * domain relevance | * decision context
- user | **Student** | | * knowledge transfer through PRs | * defending decisions | * critiquing peer work
:::

::: {.fa-card cols=2}
- clock | **Progression over time** | | * align within teams | * cross-team review | * industry validation
- network-wired | **Audience shift** | from internal review to external scrutiny
:::

::: {.notes}
Feedback comes from **three sources**, each looking at something different — and the **audience widens deliberately** over the semester.

- **Instructor** → method and implementation
- **Industry** → domain relevance and decision context
- **Students** → defending their own decisions, critiquing peers
- Scrutiny increases: within team → cross-team → **industry validation**
- Full technical implementation is **tomorrow's talk**
:::

---

## Credibility and trust

::: {.fa-card cols=1}
- shield | **Industry threshold** | credibility is fragile: early issues can undermine trust in the entire result
- clipboard-list | **What is evaluated** | | * reproducibility | * transparency | * clear assumptions
- lightbulb | **Shift in communication** | build trust first — then invite inspection
:::

::: {.lean-in}
Students are told to **present to sell** — then invite the partner to read the codebase if curious, and the final report if they want the full picture. _(The report is what I grade — the partner never needs to.)_
:::

::: {.notes}
Let me give you a concrete example of why **credibility matters** more than you might expect.

- Our industry partner found **one error** early in a student presentation
- The rest of the work was solid — but they **lost trust in all of it** and stopped engaging
- That experience drove the shift: **build trust first**, then invite scrutiny
- **Reproducible workflows** are how you make credibility visible — not just claimed
:::



---

## Indicative evidence

::: {.fa-card cols=1}
- user-graduate | **Students & graduates** | | * high engagement without attendance requirements | * transfer of reproducible workflows | * stronger documentation practices
- building-columns | **Academic perspective** | more ambitious, student-driven projects
- industry | **Industry perspective** | improved coherence and credibility; results could be implemented in practice
:::

::: {.notes}
This has only been run **once** — so take it as a **design case**, not a controlled study. But the early signal is **promising across all three groups**.

- Students: **no attendance requirement**, yet highest attendance of any course I teach this cohort
- Graduates: they **transfer the workflows** — reproducible practices and structured documentation, years later
- Industry: outputs are **more coherent and usable** — results that could actually be implemented
- Personally — this is the **most enjoyable course I teach**. I'm just a **facilitator**; the students do the work. They're also the cohort most likely to knock on my door for advice — that says something.
:::

---

## What this design makes visible

<span class="slide-subtitle">Transferable principles for CDIO-aligned, industry-engaged courses.</span>

::: {.fa-card cols=3}
- [hi-teal] rotate-left | **Sustained context** | one domain, all semester — depth over breadth
- [hi-teal] arrows-rotate | **Assessment as iteration** | same rubric, feedback carried forward — not reset
- [hi-teal] eye | **Visible contributions** | accountability without fragmenting the team
- [hi-teal] users | **Widening audience** | from internal review to industry scrutiny
- [hi-teal] layer-group | **Accumulated feedback** | unresolved issues stay visible across submissions
- [hi-teal] screwdriver-wrench | **Tooling as support** | design comes first — tools enable it
:::

::: {.notes}
These six principles apply beyond **Business Intelligence** — the domain matters less than the design.

- Students often find this course **uncomfortable in the moment** — but graduates tell me it's where they **learned the most**, because of the hands-on experience
- They see it when applying for jobs: the skills are real and they know it
- Critical feedback, visible work, structured workflow: better to meet them first **in a course** than on the job
:::

---

## Questions? {.focus-slide}

<!-- ── Backup slide ─────────────────────────────────────── -->

::: {.two-col}
::: {}
![](include/vr2.jpg){.contact-photo}
:::

::: {}
```{=html}
{{< include partials/contact-card.html >}}
```
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
I'm happy to take questions — and if **limitations** come up: yes, it depends on **student readiness**, it scales to around **15 students**, and domain transferability is still open.

- Active refinement: **strengthen the initial phase** — more time to build trust before methods get complex
- Tomorrow, **June 24 at 1pm** — Session 8B: Learning Environments, Labs and Simulations — the technical side: *"Using Pull Requests to Make Collaboration Visible"*
:::

---

## Team and individual accountability

::: {.fa-card cols=2}
- users | **Team-level** | | * analytical quality | * coherence | * relevance
- user-check | **Individual-level** | | * visible artifacts | * documented contributions | * structured reflection
:::

::: {.statement}
Design goal: **accountability without fragmenting teamwork** — designed into the course architecture, not added after the fact.
:::

::: {.notes}
The classic tension is **teamwork versus individual accountability** — and the usual fix of splitting tasks into isolated pieces breaks collaboration.

- Our approach: keep the team together but make **contributions visible**
- PRs show who did what, how they engaged with feedback, how their thinking evolved
- **Accountability designed in** — not bolted on after the fact
- Full detail in tomorrow's talk
:::
